era_context: The Late Roman Empire under Theodosius I, consolidating power after the civil war with Maximus (383–388). The Eastern Empire was increasingly Christianized and bureaucratized. The Western Empire faced mounting pressure from Germanic peoples and internal factionalism. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty was expanding. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization continued its decline.

events.title: Theodosius I consolidates power after civil war with Maximus
events.description: Following his victory over the usurper Maximus in 388 CE (but the consolidation process extended through 384–385), Theodosius I worked to stabilize the empire and assert control over both Eastern and Western halves. In 384, Theodosius was solidifying his authority, reorganizing the military, and beginning to enforce religious orthodoxy. This period saw the rise of Ambrose of Milan as a key advisor and the increasing influence of the Christian church in imperial policy. Theodosius's reign marked a turning point toward a more explicitly Christian empire.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (though written earlier, covers this period)

events.certainty_note: The broad political situation is clear, but specific events in 384 are not sharply documented. The consolidation process extended over several years.

events.title: Jerome begins translation of the Bible into Latin (Vulgate)
events.description: Jerome, a Christian scholar and ascetic, began his monumental task of translating the Hebrew Bible and Greek New Testament into Latin. This work, which would become known as the Vulgate, was commissioned by Pope Damasus I. Jerome's translation was revolutionary—it aimed to replace the older, inconsistent Latin translations (the Old Latin versions) with a standardized, accurate text. The Vulgate would become the dominant biblical text in Western Christianity for over a thousand years and profoundly shaped Christian theology, liturgy, and culture. Jerome's work exemplified the intellectual resources the Christian church was mobilizing.

sources.name: Jerome, Prefaces to the Vulgate and Letters
Jerome, Life by Gennadius of Marseille (5th century)

events.certainty_note: Jerome's work on the Vulgate began in the 380s, with 384 being a conventional date for the start. The exact chronology is uncertain, as Jerome worked on different books at different times. The project extended over decades.
cross_references: 405-001 (Completion of major portions of the Vulgate)

events.title: Petition of the Altar of Victory controversy in Rome
events.description: Pagan senators in Rome, led by Symmachus, petitioned the young Western Emperor Valentinian II to restore the Altar of Victory in the Senate house, which had been removed by Christian emperors. The altar was a symbol of pagan religious tradition and Roman military success. Symmachus's petition was eloquent and politically sophisticated, appealing to tradition and religious tolerance. However, Bishop Ambrose of Milan vigorously opposed the petition, arguing that pagan symbols had no place in a Christian empire. The petition was denied, marking a symbolic victory for Christian dominance and the marginalization of pagan institutions in the imperial center.

sources.name: Ambrose of Milan, Letters 17–18 (response to the petition)

events.certainty_note: The petition and responses are preserved in primary sources. The date is well-established as 384 CE.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty expands military campaigns in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty continued military campaigns to consolidate control over northern China. In this period, the Wei were engaged in conflicts with rival powers and in the process of expanding their territorial base. These campaigns were part of the longer process of unification that would culminate in the Wei's control of all of northern China by the early 5th century. The military campaigns were accompanied by administrative reforms and the gradual adoption of Chinese governmental structures.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Northern Wei expansion is clear, but specific events in 384 are not well-documented in surviving sources. Dating relies on later compilations.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Jerome's Vulgate translation is uncertain. Some scholars place the beginning of his work in the late 380s rather than 384. The exact chronology of his translation of different biblical books is debated. The Altar of Victory petition is sometimes interpreted as less significant than traditional accounts suggest—it may have been a rhetorical exercise rather than a serious political threat. The extent of Northern Wei military activity in 384 specifically is unclear from surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The record for 384 CE is dominated by Roman and Christian sources, with a strong bias toward elite political and religious events. The sources are primarily from the Eastern Empire and Rome itself. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. The perspectives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. Archaeological evidence is increasingly important but unevenly distributed.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius's consolidation of power enabled his anti-pagan legislation
Part of the broader conflict between paganism and Christianity
Jerome's Vulgate translation project extended over decades
